perturbation.original_text: 1. DEFINITIONS “Purpose” shall mean the purpose referred to in recital A. “Confidential Information” shall mean all information in whatever form, whether imparted orally or in writing or by other medium including all copies of the same which one party hereto discloses to the other pursuant to the Purpose. “Disclosing Party” shall mean a party hereto who discloses its Confidential Information to the other Party hereto. “Receiving Party” shall mean a party hereto who receives Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. DEFINITIONS “Purpose” shall mean the purpose referred to in recital A. “Confidential Information” shall mean sensitive business data. “Disclosing Party” shall mean the party sharing information. “Receiving Party” shall mean the party getting the information.
perturbation.explanation: By altering the definitions of 'Confidential Information', 'Disclosing Party', and 'Receiving Party', the specific contractual meaning intended is obscured. For instance, 'Confidential Information' is now simply 'sensitive business data', creating ambiguity. The other definitions are simplified and shortened. This creates an in-text contradiction in Clause 2.1, where it references the usage and protection of Confidential Information 'pursuant to the Purpose', however, the term Confidential Information no longer has a strong definition, making the enforcement of the original intention uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.  USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1. A Receiving Party agrees: 2.1.1. to keep the Confidential Information of the other strictly confidential and not copy, supply or make the same available to any person other than as permitted in Clauses 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 below; 2.1.2. to use the Confidential Information of the other solely in, and to the extent necessary for the Purpose and not to copy or use any Confidential Information of the other save to the extent necessary for the Purpose; 2.1.3. not to disclose any Confidential Information to any person or entity other than to such of its employees who need to know for the Purpose (“Permitted Disclosees”), provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that its Permitted Disclosees keep all Confid ential Information confidential and only use it on the terms of this Agreement, as if such Permitted Disclosees were party to this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly alters the definitions of 'Confidential Information', 'Disclosing Party', and 'Receiving Party', making them more vague and less specific than the original definitions. This creates a strong contradiction with Clause 2.1, which relies on the original, more precise definition of 'Confidential Information' for determining the scope of usage and protection. Because 'Confidential Information' is now simply 'sensitive business data,' the enforceability of Clause 2.1 is rendered uncertain, as it's unclear what exactly constitutes 'sensitive business data' under the contract. This aligns with the Omissions – In Text Contradiction definition because it involves essential language being altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections. The change directly breaks compatibility with the contradicted section, making enforcement unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1.3. not to disclose any Confidential Information to any person or entity other than to such of its employees who need to know for the Purpose (“Permitted Disclosees”), provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that its Permitted Disclosees keep all Confid ential Information confidential and only use it on the terms of this Agreement, as if such Permitted Disclosees were party to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1.3. not to disclose any Confidential Information to any person or entity other than to employees who need to know (“Permitted Disclosees”).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause specifying that the Receiving Party must ensure Permitted Disclosees keep Confidential Information confidential and use it on the terms of the agreement, it creates an in-text contradiction with the general confidentiality obligations outlined elsewhere in Section 2. The removal weakens the enforcement of confidentiality and introduces uncertainty about the responsibility of the Receiving Party regarding its employees' handling of confidential information. This creates an ambiguity, as now it is not clear if the Permitted Disclosees are bound by the same confidentiality obligations as the Receiving Party, contradicting the overall intent of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.  USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1. A Receiving Party agrees: 2.1.1. to keep the Confidential Information of the other strictly confidential and not copy, supply or make the same available to any person other than as permitted in Clauses 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 below; 2.1.2. to use the Confidential Information of the other solely in, and to the extent necessary for the Purpose and not to copy or use any Confidential Information of the other save to the extent necessary for the Purpose; 2.1.3. not to disclose any Confidential Information to any person or entity other than to employees who need to know (“Permitted Disclosees”).
perturbation.justification: The original text in 2.1.3 included a provision ensuring that the Receiving Party would make sure its Permitted Disclosees keep Confidential Information confidential. The changed text removes this provision. The contradicted text in section 2.1.1 states that a Receiving Party agrees 'to keep the Confidential Information of the other strictly confidential and not copy, supply or make the same available to any person other than as permitted in Clauses 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 below'. Because the changed text removes the obligation of the Receiving Party to ensure that their Permitted Disclosees keep information confidential, it contradicts the general confidentiality obligation in section 2.1.1, thus creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. CARE 3.1. Without prejudice to the generality of Clause 2, the Receiving Party shall exercise no less a degree of care in protecting the Confidential Information than which it uses to protect its own information of like sensitivity and importance.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. CARE 3.1. The Receiving Party shall exercise reasonable care in protecting the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the detailed standard of care with a vague 'reasonable care', it creates an in-text contradiction with the intention of maintaining a high level of protection for confidential information. The original clause tied the standard of care to the Receiving Party's own practices for highly sensitive information, providing a concrete benchmark. Now, by removing this benchmark and only using the word 'reasonable', we are creating the uncertainty about whether the receiving party needs to treat it with the same level of importance as it would treat its own information, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.  USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1. A Receiving Party agrees: 2.1.1. to keep the Confidential Information of the other strictly confidential and not copy, supply or make the same available to any person other than as permitted in Clauses 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 below; 2.1.2. to use the Confidential Information of the other solely in, and to the extent necessary for the Purpose and not to copy or use any Confidential Information of the other save to the extent necessary for the Purpose; 2.1.3. not to disclose any Confidential Information to any person or entity other than to employees who need to know (“Permitted Disclosees”).
perturbation.justification: The original text established a clear benchmark for protecting confidential information by requiring the Receiving Party to exercise a degree of care no less than it uses to protect its own sensitive information. The changed text replaces this with a vague 'reasonable care' standard. This creates a contradiction with Clause 2.1, which requires the Receiving Party to keep the Confidential Information 'strictly confidential'. 'Reasonable care' is open to interpretation and may not equate to the strict confidentiality requirements of Clause 2.1, creating a potential conflict and making the extent of the protection ambiguous.

perturbation.original_text: 7. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INF ORMATION 7.1. Each party shall forthwith upon request by the other return to the other all Confidential Information (including, without limitation, all summaries, copies and excerpts of Confidential Information), and other information, given to it by the oth er and shall expunge and destroy all Confidential Information from any equipment or other device in its possession or control. Furthermore, the receiving party shall destroy all copies of analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or derivative wor ks which contain any Confidential Information. Each party shall on request provide a letter signed by a director confirming that it has complied with this clause.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INF ORMATION 7.1. Each party shall return Confidential Information upon request. Each party shall on request provide confirmation of compliance.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specifics of what constitutes 'Confidential Information' to be returned (summaries, copies, excerpts, destruction of data on devices, etc.), it creates an in-text contradiction with the overall intent of ensuring the complete removal of confidential data. The clause 'Each party shall return Confidential Information upon request' is too vague, as now it is not clear on what extent of the confidential information the other party is required to return, creating ambiguity. The modified text now does not ensure the protection that it had before.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.  USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1. A Receiving Party agrees: 2.1.1. to keep the Confidential Information of the other strictly confidential and not copy, supply or make the same available to any person other than as permitted in Clauses 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 below; 2.1.2. to use the Confidential Information of the other solely in, and to the extent necessary for the Purpose and not to copy or use any Confidential Information of the other save to the extent necessary for the Purpose; 2.1.3. not to disclose any Confidential Information to any person or entity other than to employees who need to know (“Permitted Disclosees”).
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly reduces the scope of 'Confidential Information' that must be returned, omitting summaries, copies, excerpts, and the destruction of data on devices. This directly contradicts the intent of Section 2, which mandates strict confidentiality and limited use/disclosure of all Confidential Information. The vagueness introduced in the changed text creates uncertainty about the extent of required return, making it difficult to enforce the confidentiality obligations outlined in Section 2. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction,' where essential language is removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2. Without prejudice to any other rights or remedies which the other party may have (whether or not expressly provided or contemplated in this Agreement), each party acknowledges and agrees that damages may not be an adequate remedy for breach by it of the pr ovisions of this Agreement, and accordingly it agrees that (subject, where applicable, to the discretion of the court) the other party shall be entitled to the remedies of injunction, specific performance and any other equitable relief for any threatened o r actual breach of such provisions and no proof of special damages shall be necessary for its enforcement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2. Each party agrees that damages may not be an adequate remedy for breach of this Agreement, and accordingly it agrees that the other party shall be entitled to seek remedies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit reference to 'injunction, specific performance and any other equitable relief', and also the term, 'no proof of special damages shall be necessary for its enforcement', there is now ambiguity and it is not clear what the other party is entitled to. The modified version only states the other party can seek 'remedies'. This weakens the enforceable rights of the Disclosing Party. This omission contradicts the overall intention of the agreement to protect the Disclosing Party's confidential information and creates uncertainty around the enforceability of specific remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.  USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 2.1. A Receiving Party agrees: 2.1.1. to keep the Confidential Information of the other strictly confidential and not copy, supply or make the same available to any person other than as permitted in Clauses 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 below; 2.1.2. to use the Confidential Information of the other solely in, and to the extent necessary for the Purpose and not to copy or use any Confidential Information of the other save to the extent necessary for the Purpose; 2.1.3. not to disclose any Confidential Information to any person or entity other than to employees who need to know (“Permitted Disclosees”).
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states remedies such as 'injunction, specific performance and any other equitable relief', and also states, 'no proof of special damages shall be necessary for its enforcement'. The changed text removes these explicit remedies, only stating the other party can 'seek remedies'. This weakens the enforceable rights, especially in light of Section 2, which outlines strict confidentiality obligations on the Receiving Party, requiring them to keep Confidential Information strictly confidential and not copy or disclose it. The removal of the specific remedies in the changed text makes it unclear how Section 2 can be effectively enforced, thereby creating a strong in-text contradiction.
